systems.name: Modal Derivation System
systems.summary: The central system of the book: every chord quality is derived by extracting numbered scale tones from a named parent mode. Major chord types share the major scale of the chord root; dominant seventh and ninth share the Mixolydian mode; all four minor chord qualities share the Dorian mode; diminished chords derive from the diminished scale; augmented chords from the whole-tone scale. The system uses formulaic extraction, the parent-key relationship (not interval measurement), and the IIm7-V7-IM7 progression as the primary traversal axis.

members.name: Major Family (Ionian/Major Scale)
members.summary: Major Triad (1-3-5), Major Sixth (1-3-5-6), Major Seventh (1-3-5-7), Major Ninth (1-3-5-7-9) — all sharing the major scale of the chord root.

members.name: Dominant Family (Mixolydian Mode)
members.summary: Dominant Seventh (1-3-5-7) and Dominant Ninth (1-3-5-7-9) — both sharing the same Mixolydian mode.

members.name: Minor Family (Dorian Mode)
members.summary: Minor Triad, Minor Sixth, Minor Seventh, Minor Ninth — all sharing the Dorian mode of the chord root.

members.name: Diminished Family (Diminished Scale)
members.summary: Diminished Triad and Diminished Seventh, sharing the diminished scale on the chord root.

members.name: Whole-Tone Family
members.summary: Augmented Triad and Augmented Seventh Chord, derived from the whole-tone scale.

members.name: Augmented Scale Family
members.summary: M7+5 (primary) and M7 (secondary) chords, derived from the augmented scale.

members.name: Lydian Augmented Scale Family
members.summary: Applied to dominant seventh chords via two root-alignment positions.

traversal_rules.name: IIm7-V7-IM7 Chord-Group Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Navigate the IIm7-V7-IM7 progression as a single harmonic unit sharing one parent key signature.

references.quote_excerpt: the scales of these three chords will share exactly the same key signature
an improviser who can read groups of chords that are related to each other, rather than improvising on one chord at a time

traversal_rules.name: Shared Parent Key Continuity (IIm7-V7)
traversal_rules.summary: Because IIm7 and V7 share the same parent key, a single scalar pattern begun on the IIm7 may continue unaltered through the V7.

references.topic: Shared parent key / common scale rule
references.quote_excerpt: The pattern fits both chords because they both have the same parent key.
these tones also fit the dominant seventh chord because both chords use the same "parent" scale

traversal_rules.name: Directional Alternation Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Systematically alternate ascending and descending directions across successive repetitions to build symmetrical command.

references.quote_excerpt: Apply the same principle of alternating the ascending and descending forms to the chords in patterns No. 2, 3 and 4.
Apply the principle of alternating the descending and ascending forms of the chord scales to the chords in patterns No. 34, 35 and 36.
ascending the scale of the minor seventh chord and descending the scale of the dominant seventh chord

traversal_rules.name: All-Twelve-Keys Mandatory Transposition
traversal_rules.summary: Every pattern must be transposed through all twelve keys; transposition is mandatory, not optional.

references.quote_excerpt: The instructions for transposition... must be followed to achieve adequate results.
Practice in all keys.

references.topic: C Diminished Scale pattern practice rule
references.quote_excerpt: Be sure to practice it using EVERY starting note illustrated on the chart in fig. 32.

modification_rules.name: Formulaic Extraction (Scale-Degree Formula -> Chord)
modification_rules.summary: Any chord is built by extracting the scale degrees named in its formula from its parent mode; no chord is memorized in isolation.

references.quote_excerpt: By extracting tones No. 1-3-5 from the C Major scale, we arrive at the notes forming the C Major Triad.
By extracting tones No. 1-3-5-7 from the B-Flat Mixolydian mode, we arrive at the notes forming the B-Flat Dominant Seventh Chord
By extracting the tones No. 1-3-5 from the B Dorian Mode, we arrive at the notes forming the B Minor Triad.
By extracting cones No. 1-3-5 from any diminished scale, we arrive at the Diminished Triad on that particular root.
By extracting the tones No. 1-3-5 from any whole tone scale, we arrive at the Augmented Triad on that particular root.

modification_rules.name: Family Unification - Single Symbol Convenience
modification_rules.summary: When multiple chord qualities share one parent scale, a single chord symbol labels all patterns in the family for notational convenience.

references.quote_excerpt: The use of the triad symbol in the following patterns is merely for convenience

references.topic: Dorian mode on minor chord types
references.quote_excerpt: The use of the minor seventh chord symbol is merely for convenience
The use of the Diminished Triad symbol in the following patterns is merely for convenience

modification_rules.name: Two-Fragment Coverage Rule
modification_rules.summary: Pair two complementary scale-degree fragments to achieve complete harmonic coverage of a chord family or progression within a single pattern.

references.quote_excerpt: This pattern utilizes two fragments (5-8-6-4 and 2-5-3-1) from the chord scale, which when played simultaneously will sound any of the major forms of the chords expressed (Triad, M6, M7 and M9).
The fragment 3-4-5-7 is to be extracted from the scale of the minor 7th chord, and the fragment 3-4-5-1 is to be extracted from the scale of the dominant seventh chord.
This pattern uses the fragments 3-4-5-7 9-10-9-8 from the scale of the minor seventh chord, and fragment 1-2-6-5

modification_rules.name: Enharmonic Equivalence / Extension Collapse
modification_rules.summary: Upper extensions collapse to equivalent lower scale degrees (9=2, 11=4, 13=6); enharmonic substitution is permitted to maintain correct chord spelling within non-diatonic scales.

references.quote_excerpt: the ninth, eleventh and thirteenth are also the second, fourth and sixth tones respectively, as they are the same letter names.
The use of enharmonic tones is justifiable whenever the student desires.

modification_rules.name: Compositional Economy - Pattern Recycling to Longer Forms
modification_rules.summary: Rather than introducing new patterns for longer progressions, extend mastered short-form vocabulary into four-measure contexts by applying it to the IM7 resolution.

references.quote_excerpt: Rather than introducing new patterns, we will use four of the patterns previously discussed with the IIm7-V7 progression and extend them to the IM7.
Rather than introducing new patterns, we will use two of the patterns previously discussed with the IIm7-V7 progression (two measures) and extend them to the IM7.

modification_rules.name: Dominant Seventh Color Tone Alteration
modification_rules.summary: Dominant seventh chords may freely alter the ninth, fifth, eleventh, and thirteenth without losing dominant function, provided the third remains major and seventh remains minor.

references.topic: Dominant function retention with altered ninths
references.quote_excerpt: the chord retains its dominant function as long as the third is major and the seventh is minor, regardless of the type of ninth used.
the fifth of a dominant seventh may be unaltered (perfect), lowered (diminished), or raised (augmented), without changing the chord's function.
the pattern also yields the color tones of flat-9, +9, +11, and 13... all four color tones are common to one another and can be used together to good effect.

systems.name: Symmetrical Scale Reduction System
systems.summary: Governs the non-diatonic scale territories: each symmetrical scale family has a fixed number of distinct pitch collections covering all twelve roots (whole-tone=2, diminished=3, augmented=4). Defines how to navigate these reduced sets, substitution licenses by shared-root principle, and the half-step offset rule for diminished-over-dominant.

members.name: Whole-Tone Scale (2 distinct collections)
members.summary: Six roots share one set; six share another.

members.name: Diminished Scale (3 distinct collections)
members.summary: Twelve roots collapse to three chromatically adjacent scales; four roots share each scale.

members.name: Augmented Scale (4 distinct collections)
members.summary: Twelve roots collapse to four scales; six tones each.

traversal_rules.name: Four-Root Diminished Symmetry Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Any of the four roots sharing a diminished scale may serve as the tonal starting point.

references.quote_excerpt: when using the tones E-flat, G-flat and A as starting points, the student is actually playing the same tones as he did when starting on C.
there are (in sound) only three different diminished scales, chromatically adjacent

traversal_rules.name: Two-Collection Whole-Tone Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: All twelve whole-tone starting points belong to one of only two pitch collections.

references.topic: Whole tone scale — only two distinct sets
references.quote_excerpt: Thus the C whole tone scale is used to form five other whole tone scales. all having the same tones but with different starting pitches.

traversal_rules.name: Four-Collection Augmented Scale Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Only four chromatically adjacent augmented scales exist; the student cycles through only four distinct scales.

references.quote_excerpt:
Only four (chro-
matically adjacent) augmented scales exist.
Also practice this pattern starting on B-natural, C, and D-flat (B augmented, C augmented, and D-flat augmented).

modification_rules.name: Diminished-Fifth (Tritone) Substitution Rule
modification_rules.summary: Dominant seventh chords whose roots lie a diminished fifth (tritone) apart, or a minor third apart within the same diminished seventh chord, may substitute for each other.

references.quote_excerpt:
chords whose roots are within the
same diminished seventh chord are likely substitutes for each other.

modification_rules.name: Diminished Scale Half-Step Rule for Dominant Chords
modification_rules.summary: Apply the diminished scale whose root lies a half-step above the dominant seventh chord root to obtain flat-9, +9, +11, and 13 color tones simultaneously.

references.topic: diminished scale over dominant seventh: half-step rule
references.quote_excerpt:
the root of the scale is a half-step above the root of the seventh chord (i.e, a C-sharp dimi-
nished scale is used with a C7).

references.topic: diminished scale fits four dominant chords
references.quote_excerpt:
a diminished scale on C-sharp, E, G, or A will fit and enhance (because of the color
tones provided) a C7, E-flat™, Fesharp7, or A? chord.

modification_rules.name: Lydian Augmented Scale Two-Position Deployment
modification_rules.summary: Apply the Lydian Augmented Scale to dominant seventh chords in two distinct root-alignment positions.

references.quote_excerpt:
It has two kinds of
application to dominant seventh chords, one resulting in the addition of a flatted fifth (or aug-
mented eleventh) and the other application supplying an augmented fifth, a flatted ninth, an aug-
mented ninth, and an augmented eleventh.
In the case of the dominant 7th, flat 5th (b 5), the root of the scale is the seventh of the chord.

In the case of the dominant 7th, raised 5th, raised 9th, lowered 9th, and augmented 11th, the root
of the scale is the third of the chord.

modification_rules.name: Chromatic Non-Harmonic Tone Insertion
modification_rules.summary: Insert chromatic passing tones between whole-step intervals in a diminished scale pattern to produce chromatic density while preserving the scale's character.

references.topic: chromatic non-harmonic tones in diminished scale
references.quote_excerpt:
Beginning with Pattern No. 216, a non-harmonic (non-chord) tone is added chromatically between
pairs of tones from the scale—pairs which are normally a whole-step apart.

modification_rules.name: Polychord Upper Structure Selection
modification_rules.summary: Choose the upper triad of a polychord either from the given chord's substitution set or by building from its 9th/11th/13th extensions while preserving the bottom chord's function.

references.topic: Two methods for choosing added triad
references.quote_excerpt:
either by using both the given chord and one of its
substitutions; or by locating a second chord whose tones are made up of ninths, elevenths, or thir-
teenths of the given chord

the added
triad is chosen so as not to change the function of the bottom (given) chord.

systems.name: Turnaround Chord-Type Permutation System
systems.summary: Compact system governing stationary-root chord-type permutation as a harmonic device. Holds the root constant while cycling through chord qualities, shifting harmonic function and key center. Distinguished from cycle-of-fifths and chromatic-descent progressions.

members.name: Tonic Major Seventh (IM7)
members.summary: Starting quality; held for approximately two beats.

members.name: Minor Seventh Quality on Same Root

traversal_rules.name: Stationary-Root Chord-Type Permutation
traversal_rules.summary: Hold the chord root constant across two or more successive chords while changing the chord quality and function.

references.quote_excerpt:
the root will remain stationary through two or more chords,
while the chord type on that same root will change.

modification_rules.name: Turnaround Endpoint Constraint
modification_rules.summary: A turnaround begins with a tonic chord (about two beats) and ends with V7 or a flat-II7/flat-IIM7 dominant substitute.

references.quote_excerpt:
most will begin with a conic chord (but only for about two beats) and wil! end
with either a V7 chord (dominant) or a flat-I17 or flat-1M7 (dominant substitute.)

a device is needed which would remove the excessive use of the tonic and at the
same time give the phrase-endings a sense of direction, namely to return gracefully to the beginning
of a repeated section.
